item.title: Junior Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image appears to originate from this site with an incompatible license. Image was removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Birds can't see in three dimensions, so how can they recognize the difference of written and carved letters? Beats me, but as stated previously, chickadees. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Automatic +1 for including Animorphs.

item.title: Why safe?
item.content:encoded: If you don't feed them, they die, so why isn't this Euclid?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like that most of the mystery remains with this one. I also agree with those that said this needed the interview log; it kinda elevates the whole thing.

Good stuff though. Hard to believe I read this in the past and didn't upvote. This has of course since been corrected.
Good going.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh hey I never noticed the Animorphs one.

What a melancholy SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is adorable

aside from them killing themselves doing it though

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the animorphs nostalgia. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I appreciate that this is one of the highest rated SCPs on the site but nevertheless I feel there are two minor points you might want to change:

1. “Occasional medical care is necessary to maintain the health of SCP-1468” - Whilst not technically incorrect, it is more common to describe veterinary medical care as “veterinary care” rather than “medical care” in order to distinguish it from human medical care.

2. “As of writing, SCP-1468-19 is the closest to completion, being approximately 78% done” – A child is “done” with his homework, in this context “finished”, “completed” or “transcribed” would be more appropriate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Another great SCP I've missed out on. Loved it, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well-written, believable, causes emotional response without trying too hard with feels and tragix baxstories. Excellent job. +1

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded: Thank you. I'm really flattered by the praise this has received.
Great job, Harland.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wish one more person had upvoted before I, its sitting at +99 right now.

This is a nice break from the more grimdark pieces on the site, but it still fits in and isn't trying too hard. I like it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I…I am so torn about this. It is adorable and sad, and just….Why must these birds suffer so!? There's almost a metaphor for artists suffering for their work here, but that's likely just reading into it far too much. Up vote commencing!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Of Mice and Men, Turn Of the Screw, and certain Shakespear plays were all very popular choices for a while. But they have such an obviously favorable prestige/length ratio that the dating scene got flooded with them. Females have come to dismiss such works as lazy and disingenuous, not a true reflection of the males taste in literature and hence suitability. Like most mating displays, it's at least as much about the effort as it is about the quality.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Green Eggs and Ham would be suitably short. Unfortunately that particular bird chose a very hard surface to write on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Under this theory, it seems the best option for these birds might be to carve up a very short literary classic such as Of Mice and Men.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So here is my personal headcanon interpretation of this SCP:

There is a strain of chickadee that has access to the sum of human literature, and the intelligence to interpret it. This isn't the fraction of a percent of the total population that is an active SCP-1468, but maybe most or all of the species can read every book ever. 'Normal' chickadees spend their entire lives doing normal chickadee things whilst simultaneously reading Shakespeare, and are content in living their short lives the way other birds do, because really what life is better than meeting a nice lady-bird, having a couple eggs, and getting old eating worms or whatever chickadees eat and reading Dostoefsky.

The individuals that the Foundation has labeled 'anomalous' are simply victims of a common chickadee mental illness: a natural response of the chickadee psychology to their unique lot in life. Chickadees can read all the great books in the world, you see, but cannot write, nor create at all. Sometimes it gets to be too much, and the bird snaps, and throws its life away trying to prove to the world that it can write too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In an AWCY? scenario, the birds would have a similar meaning to Skip, the adorable junk robot. The birds take their literary work super-serious, suffering and dieing without a second thought. But they aren't adding anything, they aren't being creative. The chickadees are harming themselves for no reason because they don't understand what constitutes "real art". The birds would be a parody of all the mainstream artists that AWCY? holds in contempt because they don't work in an "anart" medium.

It won't become my personal head-canon on this one, but I think an AWCY? interpretation hangs together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perso-Arabic script. Also, it's not "English," but "Latin" IIRC.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not even the nihilists want AWCY?.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a nihilist, I'm offended by the comparison to AWCY?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was really hoping one would be doing James Joyce's Finnegans Wake. That one would have quite a story to tell.
That aside, lovely article. I can get behind the compulsion to preserve literature.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fucking Nihilists.

Say what you will about the tenets of birds carving books, dude, at least it's an ethos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, think about it. These guys do what they do because they love books, and books have some serious meaning to them, to the point that they practically kill themselves over it.

AWCY is a bunch of pretentious nihilists who consider nothing meaningful and important.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Chickadees are the opposite of hipsters.

I learned something today.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, because AWCY are a bunch of dumb hipsters who see no reason or meaning in anything, while these guys are the exact opposite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this theory. I feel so sorry for the one that chose Insomnia.

I love the chickadees. What makes this such an amazing SCP is it's sheer believeability. Very little suspension of disbelief was required. I can almost see this happening IRL.

Hmm. Raises questions of origin. Is this a single population? It would explain the spontaneous unexplained mutation. Could be intentional/engineered. Hmm. A biological clockmaker with a literary bent? I wonder if the last thing in a manuscript is going to be "AWCY?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a great animal SCP. I love that the most sinister thing about it is that they're killing themselves; there's no higher purpose or overall scheme. I love that it's chickadees (I fucking love chickadees). I, too, love that something good happened to a Foundation employee. And On a Pale Horse was great, as I recall; I need to pick that series back up sometime.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For some reason I found this entry cute. They display odd behavior but aren't particularly aggressive or anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Random what-iffery: They haven't seen any mating behavior because story carving is their mating display, and they haven't seen a finished book yet. The female judges the male based on his pen(beak)manship, grammar, accuracy to source material, etc. Extra credit given for novel or particularly impressive materials carved. Much like some species try to impress with a nest incorporating shiny things.

The males have a dilemma when deciding what to write. Go with something quick and easy, like Animorphs, that you can scrape out in a couple months; or go for the big showy classic literature pieces that are sure to impress the chicks (lol), but are also exponentially more challenging and time consuming. War and Peace will land more tail than one bird can handle, but will you survive to complete it?

Over time, this has developed into an escalating "arms race". Each male tries to out-do the competition and pushes himself as hard as he can. But as sometimes happens with IRL mating displays, sometimes the urge to mate overwhelms the more immediate survival instincts with tragic results.

Specimens in containment are carving more because they have realized that the Foundation staff will help sustain them. They can get medical treatment to pull them back from the edge of death, which frees them up for even more impressive literary feats.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, because they couldn't carve punctuation into the blanks or the backs of the rarer letters… that would be sacrilege.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I had a little difficulty determining what the proper way to refer to that particular alphabet was, as I found different things with the same characters calling it the Farsi alphabet, Persian alphabet, and the Arabic alphabet. I fixed that.

As for the classification thing, that was a goof on my part from late-stage editing to make it more clear that it wasn't its own species, but it seems I missed the reference near the end that didn't agree with that. I'll work on fixing this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Another one: They're normally (barring complications) extremely long-lived and have highly efficient metabolisms. They only become fertile once they succeed in finishing their story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because instances of SCP-1468 rarely engage in behaviors not related to carving, no mating has been observed. Because of this, artificial means have been suggested in order to ensure that current instances of SCP-1468 produce offspring. If this does not succeed, it is believed that SCP-1468 as a species could potentially drive itself to extinction.

If the sub-species always acted like this it would never have been able to survive in the wild. Of course it might be that in the recent past an anomalous event turned the entire sub-population from ordinary to SCPs all at once, but in that case there's no guarantee that the offspring of SCP-1468 would also be anomalous.

Some other possibilities:

1) The SCPs come from all sub-populations of the species, and periodically ordinary members of Poecile atricapillus spontaneously become anomalous.

2) The SCP is a distinct species, and doesn't start the carving until it's reproduced.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
According to Wikipedia, the "Farsi alphabet" is actually the Persian alphabet, itself derived from written Arabic.

Near the end of this entry, you classify SCP-1468 as a separate species, but at the beginning of the description you call it a subpopulation. Subpopulation mostly implies a geographically separate unit while species implies little to no interbreeding, so interchanging them made the taxonomist in me twitch. My suggestion would be to change

SCP-1468 is the collective designation for a population of bird, a subset of the species Poecile atricapillus, or Black-capped Chickadee.

to something like "SCP-1468 is the collective designation for a subpopulation of black-capped chickadees (Poecile atricapillus)." Or you could just call them a flock, seeing as we probably don't know if they'll interbreed with normal chickadees.

Note that common names generally aren't capitalized unless they contain a geographic designation or are named after a person (e.g., Steller's jay).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very well done. I really enjoyed the interview. 1'ed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I appreciate it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Then it's a good thing I didn't go with the ending where the interview fails and Karen is demoted and drops into a deep depression that begins to affect her marriage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They are also hypocrites.

And yeah what Scantron said, they were in the middle of sentences when they answered. I decided not to actually include what -29 was carving from I Am Legend when it answered, because it would have read something like

"he thought to Yes himself while Yes he sat with a A story drink in his hand"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for a very original concept, and I wish I could upvote again for something good happening to a Foundation researcher for once.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Saw chickadees, double checked to make sure the article was well-written and interesting, it was, then I upvoted. There's something to be said about an idea standing on its own, but there's also something to be said for chickadees.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Presumably it was in the middle of a sentence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was a bit sceptical when you'd pitched this in chat, Harland, but you pulled it off. Good show.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And yet the interviewed bird didn't punctuate?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good work on the log. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Any particular reason you wouldn't like it otherwise?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I appreciate it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, they do still have the amount completed recorded, it just isn't in the current instance of the observation log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, Scrabble doesn't have question mark tiles, and the birds are HUGE grammar nitpickers, and would refuse to answer any questions without proper punctuation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The secret to getting upvotes is cute little birdies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just liking it is good enough for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you like it, thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a book in there for everyone, I'm hoping to get by entirely on people seeing a book they like and voting because of that,

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks a lot.
